review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: Authentication completion is duplicated across web entrypoints instead of living behind one application-service boundary.

evidence: `AuthController.verifyGoogleToken()` normalizes claims, upserts the user, and mints a JWT inline; `verifyMicrosoftToken()` repeats the same completion workflow for a different provider.

`OAuth2SuccessHandler.onAuthenticationSuccess()` repeats the normalized-identity -> `userService.findOrCreateUser()` -> `jwtTokenService.generateToken()` -> `authCodeStore.storeJwt()` sequence for server-side logins.

`AuthController` also owns Microsoft challenge-session cookie handling and auth-code exchange, making one controller the coordination point for several auth sub-protocols rather than a thin transport adapter.
detail.suggestion: Extract a provider-neutral authentication application service that owns normalized-identity completion, JWT minting, and auth-code issuance, then keep controllers and success handlers focused on HTTP/OAuth protocol translation only.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: The documented runtime provider-config boundary has drifted into a backend/frontend-specific loose shape.

evidence: The design document requires `/api/auth/providers` to return a stable schema where disabled Microsoft config is still represented explicitly with `null`/empty values.
`ProviderConfigController.getProviders()` only adds Microsoft `clientId` and `authority` when Microsoft client-side login is enabled, so disabled and enabled responses have different shapes.

`normalizeProviderConfig()` accepts partial objects and backfills defaults on the frontend, which means the consumer is compensating for a drifting boundary instead of enforcing one published contract.
detail.suggestion: Replace the stringly `Map<String, Object>` provider payload with a typed backend DTO that always emits the full schema, and have the frontend consume that exact contract without partial-object normalization.

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: Top-level docs no longer describe the application that actually runs

evidence: `README.md` contains only whitespace, so the repository has no top-level architectural or runbook entry point.
`frontend/README.md` is the untouched Vite template rather than frontend-specific guidance for this SSO app.

`docs/guides/google-sso-integration.md` still documents Google bootstrap through `VITE_GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID`, while `frontend/src/App.tsx` now wraps routes with `GoogleOAuthProvider` only after runtime provider metadata arrives.

The same Google guide says `spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto: update`, but `backend/src/main/resources/application.yml` configures `ddl-auto: validate`, so operational guidance and runtime behavior have drifted.
detail.suggestion: Replace the blank/template readmes with a short repo-specific overview and refresh the Google integration guide so it matches the current runtime contract (`/auth/providers`, current bootstrap path, and actual persistence settings).

review::.::holistic::high_level_elegance.summary: Provider ownership is scattered across framework layers instead of a clear feature slice

evidence: The backend's top-level packages under `com.demo.sso` are generic technical layers (`config`, `controller`, `model`, `repository`, `service`) rather than feature areas.

Implementing or changing Microsoft auth spans `MicrosoftAuthProperties`, `MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter`, `AuthController`, `ProviderConfigController`, `MicrosoftTokenVerifier`, and `RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore`, which distributes one provider's behavior across multiple unrelated package owners.

On the frontend, the same provider is split between `HomePage` (composition), `MicrosoftClientSideLogin` (flow), `useProviderConfig` (contract fetch), and `types/auth.ts` (public contract), so ownership is similarly reconstructed by following references instead of opening one provider-focused module.
detail.suggestion: Reorganize auth code around explicit provider-focused areas—at minimum co-locate Microsoft and Google flow wiring with their public contracts, keeping only the shared identity/JWT core in common modules—so a provider change is navigated through one slice instead of every framework layer.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: AuthController mixes anonymous map payloads with typed DTO-style endpoints

evidence: `AuthController.verifyGoogleToken(...)` and `exchangeCode(...)` accept `Map<String, String>` bodies, and multiple methods return `ResponseEntity<?>` backed by inline `Map.of(...)` payloads.

The same controller layer already uses explicit record types for Microsoft challenge/verify payloads and for `UserResponse`, so neighboring endpoints do not follow one settled boundary-contract convention.
detail.suggestion: Add small request/response records for the Google verify, code exchange, and logout payloads, and update `AuthController` to use those explicit DTO types instead of anonymous maps.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.note: AuthController uses typed DTOs: GoogleVerifyRequest, AuthCodeExchangeRequest, MicrosoftVerifyRequest - no ad hoc payloads

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified all AuthController request/response types and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.summary: ProviderConfigController publishes a stringly typed nested map contract

evidence: `ProviderConfigController.getProviders()` manually assembles nested `LinkedHashMap<String, Object>` structures with string keys such as `serverSideEnabled`, `clientSideEnabled`, `clientId`, `authority`, and `scopes`.
Because the controller package already has a `dto/` namespace for API records, this endpoint becomes an exception where developers must read implementation details to discover the response shape.
detail.suggestion: Define explicit DTO records for the provider config payload and return those types from `ProviderConfigController` so controller contracts are discoverable from types rather than map keys.

review::.::holistic::convention_outlier:.note: ProviderConfigController returns typed ProviderConfigResponse record, not an anonymous map - finding is false

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified ProviderConfigController return types and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: The shared frontend API client discards backend error messages and throws status-only errors.

evidence: `frontend/src/api/client.ts` throws `new Error(\`API error: ${response.status}\`)` for every non-OK response and never reads the response body.

`AuthController` returns specific `{error: ...}` bodies such as `Invalid Google credential`, `Invalid Microsoft credential`, `Invalid or expired code`, and `Microsoft client-side login is disabled`, but that detail is lost before any frontend caller can inspect it.
detail.suggestion: Update `apiFetch` to parse JSON/text error bodies on non-2xx responses and throw a structured error that preserves both HTTP status and the backend `error` message, then let auth callers use that preserved detail instead of status-only failures.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: client.ts:68-74 already parses error bodies via response.json() into structured ApiError with message field - not status-only

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified client.ts error handling code and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: The homepage silently swallows auth-code exchange failures at the login boundary.

evidence: `HomePage` exchanges `?code=` through `apiFetch('/auth/exchange')` and then uses `.catch(() => { /* Code was invalid or expired — stay on homepage */ })` with no log, no telemetry, and no visible error state.
That means an expired code and a network failure both collapse into the same 'nothing happened' outcome, making the failure mode impossible to distinguish during debugging or normal use.
detail.suggestion: Add local error state in `HomePage` and surface a visible message when `/auth/exchange` fails, at minimum distinguishing invalid/expired codes from transport failures instead of swallowing both silently.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: HomePage.tsx:18 has exchangeError state, lines 38-49 display error text via conditional rendering - errors are not silent

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified HomePage.tsx error handling and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Google and Microsoft client-side login cards expose failures with different strategies.

evidence: `ClientSideLogin.handleSuccess` catches `/auth/google/verify` failures and only calls `console.error('Login failed:', error)`.

`MicrosoftClientSideLogin` catches its login/verify failures and sets a user-visible `error` message, so sibling client-side providers on the same screen teach developers different expectations about how auth errors surface.
detail.suggestion: Give `ClientSideLogin` the same explicit UI error state pattern used by `MicrosoftClientSideLogin` (or move both cards to a shared auth-error presentation helper) so sibling flows report failures consistently.

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.note: GoogleClientSideLogin and MicrosoftClientSideLogin both use identical error state pattern with setError/error rendering - no divergence

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually compared both login component error patterns and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::error_consistency::.summary: Invalid legacy login-method values are silently converted to null instead of treated as errors.

evidence: `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod` returns null for blank input and also catches `IllegalArgumentException` for invalid values, returning null again.

`UserService.findOrCreateUser(String...)` passes that result into `NormalizedIdentity.google(...)`, and `applyProviderIdentityFields` simply skips `lastLoginFlow` / `loginMethod` when the flow is null, so malformed legacy input is accepted without any signal while the rest of the auth pipeline uses exceptions for invalid identity data.
detail.suggestion: Make `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod` reject unrecognized nonblank values with `IllegalArgumentException` (or return `Optional<AuthFlow>` and force `UserService` to handle invalidity explicitly) so bad legacy login-method values do not disappear silently.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::leg.summary: Google-specific names still label provider-neutral identity data across core auth APIs

evidence: `UserService.legacyGoogleCompatibilityId(...)` writes non-Google identities into `User.googleId`, so the field name no longer matches the value it carries.

`UserRepository.findByGoogleId(...)` and `JwtTokenService.generateToken(String email, String googleId)` extend that Google-only term into repository and JWT APIs that now participate in a multi-provider identity model.
`AuthenticatedUserIdentity` still exposes `legacyGoogleId`, even though that path represents the old contract shape rather than a reliably Google-only concept.
detail.suggestion: Rename the compatibility-facing identifiers to provider-neutral terms such as `legacyIdentityKey` or `legacyProviderUserId` across the entity/service/JWT/repository APIs, and keep any truly Google-specific naming only at explicit backward-compatibility boundaries like the persisted column or legacy token claim.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::log.summary: `loginMethod` is named like an auth method but stores client/server flow values

evidence: `UserService.applyProviderIdentityFields(...)` stores `identity.loginFlow().name()` via `user.setLoginMethod(...)`, so values like `SERVER_SIDE` and `CLIENT_SIDE` are written into a field called method.
`AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod(...)` converts that same persisted value back into `AuthFlow`, confirming the data represents flow/channel semantics rather than an authentication method or provider.
`UserResponse` exposes both `loginMethod` and `lastLoginFlow`, which makes it easy for readers to assume they are separate concepts when they are really overlapping flow terminology.
detail.suggestion: Rename the internal field/accessors to `loginFlow` or `authFlow`, or if the external/persisted label must remain for compatibility, isolate the legacy `loginMethod` name at the serialization boundary and use flow-specific names in the core model and service APIs.

review::.::holistic::naming_quality::mic.summary: `isConfigured()` obscures whether Microsoft auth is client-side or server-side ready

evidence: `MicrosoftAuthProperties` defines both `isClientSideConfigured()` and `isServerSideConfigured()`, but the generic `isConfigured()` just aliases the client-side check.
`MicrosoftTokenVerifier.buildDecoder(...)` calls `properties.isConfigured()`, so readers have to open the implementation to learn which configuration level that generic name actually means.
detail.suggestion: Replace `isConfigured()` with a name that states the exact readiness level it represents, such as `isTokenVerificationConfigured()`, or call the explicit client/server-specific predicate directly at each use site.

review::.::holistic::abstraction_fitness.summary: AuthenticatedUserIdentity mixes legacy and v2 identities in one nullable record

evidence: `AuthenticatedUserIdentity.legacy(...)` creates a record with null `userId`, `provider`, `providerUserId`, and `contractVersion`, while `v2(...)` nulls `legacyGoogleId`, so valid state depends on knowing which factory built the instance rather than on the type itself.

`JwtTokenService.parseAuthenticatedUser(...)` returns both legacy and v2 identities through that same record, and `UserService.findCurrentUser(...)` has to branch on `identity.isLegacy()` to decide whether email or user ID is authoritative.

`UserController.getCurrentUser(...)` fabricates a legacy `AuthenticatedUserIdentity` from `authentication.getName()` when the principal is untyped, which shows the abstraction leaking across the controller/service boundary instead of presenting one stable principal contract.
detail.suggestion: Split the principal model into two explicit identity types behind a tiny common interface, or at minimum split the downstream lookup APIs so callers do not pass around a record whose meaningful fields depend on `contractVersion` and null sentinels.

review::.::holistic::dependency_health::.summary: Google verification ties the auth flow to the broad google-api-client library

evidence: `backend/pom.xml` adds `com.google.api-client:google-api-client` as a dedicated runtime dependency for Google token verification.
`GoogleTokenVerifier.java` uses that library only to build `GoogleIdTokenVerifier` and returns `GoogleIdToken.Payload` directly to callers.
`AuthController.java` imports `com.google.api.client.googleapis.auth.oauth2.GoogleIdToken` and works with the vendor payload in web-layer code, so the dependency is not confined to a single adapter.
By contrast, `MicrosoftTokenVerifier.java` translates vendor JWT data into app-local `NormalizedIdentity`, showing the Google path is more dependency-coupled than the rest of the auth stack.
detail.suggestion: Keep Google SDK usage inside `GoogleTokenVerifier` by translating verified claims into an app-local DTO or `NormalizedIdentity` before returning, and when this path is next touched evaluate replacing `google-api-client` with a narrower Google token-verification library so the auth subsystem depends on less vendor surface area.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: OAuth2 identity normalization hides redirect side effects behind a null sentinel

evidence: `onAuthenticationSuccess` calls `normalizeIdentity(authentication, oAuth2User, response)` and immediately returns when the helper yields `null`.

`normalizeIdentity(...)` sends redirects on its failure paths (`?error=missing_attributes`, `?error=email_not_verified`) and then returns `null`, so the caller has to know that `null` means the HTTP response has already been committed.
detail.suggestion: Keep the redirect side effects in `onAuthenticationSuccess` and make `normalizeIdentity` either return a `NormalizedIdentity` or throw for invalid claims; that removes the implicit `null means response handled` protocol from the local control flow.

review::.::holistic::low_level_elegance:.summary: Microsoft issuer and tenant checks are duplicated across verifier and normalizer

evidence: `MicrosoftTokenVerifier.validateIssuer` reconstructs `https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tid}/v2.0` from the token and throws when `iss` does not match it.
`ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeMicrosoftClaims` repeats the same `iss`/`tid`/expected-issuer validation before it starts mapping the claims into a `NormalizedIdentity`.
A reader has to reconcile the same low-level invariant in two neighboring classes instead of finding one authoritative validation point for Microsoft claim shape.
detail.suggestion: Move the shared issuer/tenant validation into one helper or keep it solely in `MicrosoftTokenVerifier`, leaving `ProviderIdentityNormalizer` focused on mapping already-validated claims into domain identity data.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Microsoft client-side auth config stops short of the actual redirect target

evidence: `ProviderConfigController.getProviders()` exposes Microsoft `clientId`, `authority`, and `scopes`, but `ProviderConfigResponse.MicrosoftProviderConfig` has no redirect URI field.
`frontend/src/types/auth.ts` mirrors that contract exactly, so the frontend has no backend-owned redirect target to consume.
`MicrosoftClientSideLogin` creates MSAL with `redirectUri: window.location.origin`, making the callback URL depend on the serving origin instead of the published provider configuration.
detail.suggestion: Extend the `/auth/providers` Microsoft contract to publish the redirect URI (or an equivalent backend-owned callback descriptor) and have `MicrosoftClientSideLogin` consume that shared value so every environment derives the same MSAL configuration from one seam.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Backend redirect errors for server-side auth are never consumed by the homepage

evidence: `OAuth2SuccessHandler` redirects failed OAuth logins to `/?error=email_not_verified` and `/?error=missing_attributes`.
`MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter` redirects disabled Microsoft callbacks to `/?error=provider_disabled&provider=microsoft&flow=server_side`.
`HomePage` only reads `searchParams.get('code')`; there is no frontend handling for `error`, `provider`, or `flow`, so those backend outcomes never become a user-visible auth result.
detail.suggestion: Define one shared server-side auth result contract: either parse and display the backend's error query parameters in `HomePage` or funnel both success and failure redirects through a single helper so the frontend handles auth callback outcomes in one place.

review::.::holistic::mid_level_elegance:.summary: Microsoft flow availability is recomputed separately at multiple boundaries

evidence: `ProviderConfigController` decides Microsoft availability by combining rollout flags with `isClientSideConfigured()` and `isServerSideConfigured()`.

`AuthController` separately checks `rolloutProperties.getMicrosoft().isClientSideEnabled()` before challenge/verify and also treats `ObjectProvider<MicrosoftTokenVerifier>` absence as another disablement path.
`MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter` and `IdentityContractGuard` each repeat their own Microsoft enablement/configuration checks, so rollout semantics live in several boundary classes.
detail.suggestion: Move Microsoft availability decisions into one shared policy on the existing config layer (for example canonical `isClientSideAvailable()` / `isServerSideAvailable()` helpers) and have the controller, filter, guard, and provider-config endpoint consume that shared verdict instead of re-deriving it.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::brow.summary: Browser-side Google and Microsoft sign-in success paths are not exercised end to end

evidence: `ClientSideLogin.tsx` converts a Google SDK credential into `POST /auth/google/verify` and then calls `onSuccess(data.token)`, but the frontend suite never drives a successful Google callback; the only Google browser assertions in `homepage.spec.ts` are card visibility, while `api-health.spec.ts` only probes the backend endpoint directly.

`MicrosoftClientSideLogin.tsx` chains challenge issuance, `loginPopup`, `/auth/microsoft/verify`, and `onSuccess`, yet `homepage.spec.ts` only checks the popup URL parameters and then expects the failure alert after the popup is closed, so the successful browser-side Microsoft path is never validated.
detail.suggestion: Add deterministic browser-level tests for both client-side login cards that stub the provider SDK success callbacks and `/auth/*/verify` responses, then assert JWT persistence and navigation to `/dashboard`.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::micr.summary: The Microsoft redirect-uri test depends on a real third-party popup URL

evidence: In `homepage.spec.ts`, the Microsoft test stubs `/api/auth/providers` and `/api/auth/microsoft/challenge`, then waits for a popup URL under `login.microsoftonline.com/.../oauth2/v2.0/authorize` and inspects its query string.

Because the authorize page itself is not stubbed and the test relies on the popup reaching a live Microsoft host, the assertion is sensitive to external network availability and third-party behavior instead of staying fully local and deterministic.
detail.suggestion: Replace the live popup dependency with an MSAL stub or another local seam around `loginPopup`, so the test can assert the redirect URI and nonce without contacting `login.microsoftonline.com`.

review::.::holistic::test_strategy::secu.summary: SecurityConfig tests are tightly coupled to Spring builder internals instead of security behavior

evidence: `SecurityConfigTest.java` creates `HttpSecurity` with `RETURNS_DEEP_STUBS`, stubs each fluent method to return itself, and then verifies that methods like `csrf`, `oauth2Login`, and `addFilterBefore` were called.

The same file also spends tests on null-constructor acceptance and multiple-instance creation, but none of those assertions would fail if `SecurityConfig` accidentally changed a route authorization rule or the unauthorized response contract.
detail.suggestion: Replace the deep-stubbed unit test with HTTP-behavior assertions similar to the existing MockMvc security tests, focusing on public/authenticated routes and unauthorized responses, and remove the constructor/null-instance checks.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: `AuthController` hides endpoint contracts behind wildcard response types

evidence: `AuthController.verifyGoogleToken`, `issueMicrosoftChallenge`, `verifyMicrosoftToken`, `exchangeCode`, and `logout` all declare `ResponseEntity<?>`, even though their success bodies are concrete DTOs such as `TokenResponse`, `MicrosoftChallengeResponse`, and `LogoutResponse`.

Neighboring controllers publish typed signatures instead (`ProviderConfigController.getProviders(): ResponseEntity<ProviderConfigResponse>` and `UserController.getCurrentUser(): ResponseEntity<UserResponse>`), so wildcard responses are not a consistent boundary convention.
detail.suggestion: Give each `AuthController` handler a concrete success return type and route shared error serialization through one explicit mechanism (for example a common exception mapper or clearly documented `ErrorResponse` contract) so the controller signatures advertise the normal response shape.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.note: AuthController uses ResponseEntity<AuthApiResponse> not ResponseEntity<?> - typed response already in place

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified AuthController.java return types and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: Provider verification APIs expose three different contract layers for the same job

evidence: `GoogleTokenVerifier.verify(String)` returns the vendor-specific `GoogleIdToken.Payload`, and callers then unpack fields to call `ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeGoogleClaims(String googleId, String email, String name, String pictureUrl, AuthFlow loginFlow)`.

`MicrosoftTokenVerifier.verifyIdToken(String credential, String expectedNonce, AuthFlow authFlow)` already returns `NormalizedIdentity`, while `ProviderIdentityNormalizer.normalizeMicrosoftClaims(Map<String, Object> claims, AuthFlow loginFlow)` accepts a raw claims map on other code paths.

Similar 'verify an identity and hand it to user creation' flows therefore make callers remember three different public contract shapes: provider payload, raw claim map, and already-normalized domain object.
detail.suggestion: Choose one public contract level for provider adapters—either have both verifiers return `NormalizedIdentity`, or have both expose typed raw claims and let one normalizer own the mapping—so Google and Microsoft flows can be used with the same mental model.

review::.::holistic::api_surface_coheren.summary: `JwtTokenService.generateToken` overloads hide which token schema a caller receives

evidence: `JwtTokenService.generateToken(String email, String googleId)` always builds the legacy subject/`googleId` token, while `generateToken(User user)` conditionally emits V2 claims or delegates back to the legacy overload depending on rollout settings.

The shared method name `generateToken` does not tell callers whether the result will contain legacy `googleId` claims or V2 `ver`/`provider`/`providerUserId`/`email` claims, and production callers use a different overload than several integration tests.
detail.suggestion: Split legacy and current minting into explicitly named entrypoints (for example `generateLegacyToken(...)` and `generateUserToken(...)`) and keep only one default public path for production callers so the token contract is obvious from the call site.

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.summary: Google disabled-state enforcement is weaker than Microsoft's on sibling public auth routes

evidence: `SecurityConfig` makes all `/oauth2/**`, `/login/oauth2/**`, and `/auth/**` endpoints public, so any provider-specific disable behavior must come from endpoint-level gates rather than the route table.

`AuthController.verifyGoogleToken()` only validates that the credential is nonblank before attempting verification, while the sibling Microsoft endpoints explicitly return `503` when `rolloutProperties.getMicrosoft().isClientSideEnabled()` is false.

`MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter` blocks `/oauth2/authorization/microsoft` and `/login/oauth2/code/microsoft` when Microsoft server-side auth is disabled, and `IdentityContractGuard` also rejects invalid Microsoft-enabled startup states.

`ProviderConfigController` can publish Google as unavailable when `googleClientId` is blank, but there is no equivalent Google request-time or startup guard to keep the public Google auth routes aligned with that advertised disabled state.
detail.suggestion: Derive provider availability from one shared fail-closed check and apply it consistently to both Google and Microsoft auth entry points. At minimum, guard the public Google auth endpoints (`/auth/google/verify` and any Google OAuth redirect entrypoint) with the same disabled/configured policy that `GET /auth/providers` publishes, returning a consistent disabled response instead of relying on downstream verifier behavior.

review::.::holistic::authorization_consi.note: Added Google rollout guard to AuthController.verifyGoogleToken — mirrors Microsoft's pattern checking rolloutProperties.getGoogle().isClientSideEnabled(). Added Google section to AuthRolloutProperties with clientSideEnabled defaulting true

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually modified AuthController.java and AuthRolloutProperties.java and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::ai_generated_debt::.summary: Expected auth failures still dump raw errors and code fragments to logs

evidence: frontend/src/components/ClientSideLogin.tsx:40 logs the entire caught error with console.error('Login failed:', error) before showing a sanitized message to the user.
frontend/src/components/MicrosoftClientSideLogin.tsx:87 repeats the same raw-object console dump with console.error('Microsoft login failed:', loginError).
backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/controller/AuthController.java:179 logs the first eight characters of an invalid auth code on the routine bad-request path.
detail.suggestion: Remove the raw object and auth-code fragment logging from these expected failure paths, or replace them with a short sanitized message that is only emitted behind development-only diagnostics if local troubleshooting truly needs it.

review::.::holistic::ai_generated_debt::.summary: ProviderConfigController null-checks googleClientId after normalizing it non-null

evidence: backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/controller/ProviderConfigController.java:28 stores googleClientId as googleClientId == null ? "" : googleClientId, establishing a non-null invariant for the field.

backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/controller/ProviderConfigController.java:33 still computes googleConfigured with googleClientId != null && !googleClientId.isBlank(), so the null half of the guard can never fire.
detail.suggestion: Keep the constructor normalization and simplify the later check to !googleClientId.isBlank(), so the code trusts the invariant it already established instead of layering another defensive branch on top.

review::.::holistic::incomplete_migratio.summary: Old `loginMethod` and new `lastLoginFlow` contracts still coexist for the same concept.

evidence: `UserService.applyProviderIdentityFields(...)` sets both `user.setLastLoginFlow(identity.loginFlow())` and `user.setLoginMethod(identity.loginFlow().name())`, so the old and new fields are populated from the same source.
`UserResponse.from(...)` exposes both `loginMethod` and `lastLoginFlow` even though they describe the same flow/channel value.
`useAuth` types only `loginMethod`, and `DashboardPage` renders only that field, so the frontend still follows the older contract while the backend also publishes the newer `lastLoginFlow` field.

The Microsoft implementation plan explicitly calls out returning `provider`, `providerUserId`, and `lastLoginFlow` from `/api/user/me`, but it does not establish `loginMethod` as part of the new provider-aware surface.
detail.suggestion: Collapse the public/current-user flow contract onto `lastLoginFlow` (or one clearly named successor), update the frontend to consume that field, and keep `loginMethod` only at a temporary serialization boundary if an external consumer still needs it; otherwise remove the duplicate field and its parser entirely.

review::.::holistic::incomplete_migratio.summary: A Google-shaped `findOrCreateUser` overload remains after production callers moved to `NormalizedIdentity`.

evidence: `AuthController` and `OAuth2SuccessHandler` both call `userService.findOrCreateUser(identity)` with a `NormalizedIdentity`, which is the provider-neutral production path.

`UserService` still keeps `findOrCreateUser(String googleId, String email, String name, String pictureUrl, String loginMethod)`, and that method does nothing except adapt into `NormalizedIdentity.google(...)`.
`AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod(...)` is only used by that legacy overload, which means the old string-based path survives mainly to support the older API shape rather than an active production need.
detail.suggestion: Remove the Google-specific string overload and route any remaining callers directly through `NormalizedIdentity` plus explicit `AuthFlow`, then delete `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod(...)` if no real production boundary still needs to parse the old field.

review::.::holistic::incomplete_migratio.summary: The legacy `google_id` compatibility column is still populated for non-Google identities.

evidence: `UserService.createNewUser(...)` and `applyProviderIdentityFields(...)` always write `user.setGoogleId(legacyGoogleCompatibilityId(identity))`.

For non-Google identities, `legacyGoogleCompatibilityId(...)` synthesizes values like `microsoft:<providerUserId>`, so the legacy column is being used as a generic compatibility bucket instead of a Google-only bridge.

Reads are already provider-aware first via `findByProviderAndProviderUserId(...)`, and the fallback `findByGoogleId(...)` is only consulted when `identity.provider() == AuthProvider.GOOGLE`, so non-Google synthetic `google_id` values are not part of the active lookup path.
The migration spec tracks dual-writing `google_id` for Google compatibility and later retirement of the column, but it does not describe storing non-Google synthetic identities in that legacy field.
detail.suggestion: Confine `google_id` writes to the actual Google-compatibility path only, stop populating synthetic non-Google values into that column, and leave the legacy field as a narrow Google bridge until the planned cleanup release removes it.

review::.::holistic::package_organizatio.summary: The flat `service/` package has become a catch-all for multiple auth concerns

evidence: The blind packet's structure profile puts `backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/service/` at 12 files, which is the only production directory in this codebase above the flat-directory overload threshold.

`service/` contains at least five different roles side by side: value records (`NormalizedIdentity`, `AuthenticatedUserIdentity`), business logic (`UserService`), Redis-backed storage adapters (`RedisAuthCodeStore`, `RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore`), verification/auth helpers (`GoogleTokenVerifier`, `MicrosoftTokenVerifier`, `JwtTokenService`, `ProviderIdentityNormalizer`), and a Spring Security callback adapter (`OAuth2SuccessHandler`).

`AuthController` imports eight different classes/interfaces from `com.demo.sso.service`, so the main auth entry point depends on a folder whose name no longer tells a reader which files are about identity models, token exchange, challenge persistence, or OAuth callbacks.

`SecurityConfig` imports `OAuth2SuccessHandler` from `service/`, which is an import-affinity mismatch: the class participates in security wiring but lives in the same flat directory as domain/business services.
detail.suggestion: Stage the reorg instead of renaming everything at once: (1) create `backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/service/identity/` and move `NormalizedIdentity`, `AuthenticatedUserIdentity`, and `ProviderIdentityNormalizer`; (2) create `backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/service/session/` and move `AuthCodeStore`, `MicrosoftChallengeStore`, `RedisAuthCodeStore`, and `RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore`; (3) create `backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso/service/auth/` and move `GoogleTokenVerifier`, `MicrosoftTokenVerifier`, `JwtTokenService`, and `OAuth2SuccessHandler`, leaving `UserService` as the only broad user-domain service. After each stage, update package declarations and imports with a Java move/rename refactor, then validate with `grep -R "import com.demo.sso.service" backend/src/main/java/com/demo/sso` to find stale flat-package imports and `cd backend && mvn -Dmaven.repo.local=/home/nt-quang/Workspaces/personal/sso_experimental/.m2/repository test` to confirm the move preserved wiring.

review::.::holistic::initialization_coup.summary: Google auth is wired as a mandatory startup dependency despite a disableable provider contract

evidence: `ProviderConfigController#getProviders()` treats a blank `googleClientId` as 'Google disabled', so the runtime contract explicitly supports a no-Google state.
`GoogleTokenVerifier` is an unconditional `@Service` whose constructor requires `@Value("${app.google-client-id}")`.
`AuthController` eagerly injects `GoogleTokenVerifier`, so the application has to construct the verifier during startup even before any Google endpoint is used.
`application.yml` defines `app.google-client-id: ${GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID}` with no default, so boot still depends on Google env being present instead of aligning with the disableable-provider contract.
detail.suggestion: Make Google wiring match the existing provider contract: give `app.google-client-id` a safe blank default, create `GoogleTokenVerifier` only when the client ID is configured (or inject it lazily via `ObjectProvider`), and return the same disabled-provider response path when Google is absent instead of failing startup.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: Microsoft challenge issuance promises replacement, but older challenges remain valid

evidence: `MicrosoftChallengeStore` documents that each session can hold one active challenge at a time and that `issueChallenge` replaces any existing challenge for the session.

`RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore.issueChallenge` stores challenges under `mschallenge:<sessionId>:<challengeId>` and never removes any earlier key for the same session, so previously issued challenge IDs remain consumable until TTL expiry.
detail.suggestion: Either enforce the documented one-active-challenge contract by tracking and invalidating the previous challenge for a session when a new one is issued, or relax the interface/Javadoc to state that multiple outstanding challenges may coexist until consumed or expired.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.note: RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore:28-30 deletes previous challenge before storing new one - replacement works correctly

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore.java lines 28-30 and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: OAuth2 success normalization advertises an identity return but uses null as failure signaling

evidence: `normalizeIdentity(Authentication, OAuth2User, HttpServletResponse)` is declared to return `NormalizedIdentity`.

On Microsoft claim validation failures, unverified Google email, and missing Google attributes, the method redirects the response and returns `null`, and `onAuthenticationSuccess(...)` must guard with `if (identity == null)` before continuing.
detail.suggestion: Make the failure channel explicit by returning `Optional<NormalizedIdentity>` or by separating validation/redirect handling so the helper either returns a valid identity or throws a clearly named exception that the caller handles.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.note: OAuth2SuccessHandler.normalizeIdentity returns Optional<NormalizedIdentity> not null - already uses proper failure signaling

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified OAuth2SuccessHandler.java return type and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.summary: Login-flow parsing silently turns invalid caller input into missing flow metadata

evidence: `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod(String)` returns `null` for blank or unsupported values instead of making failure explicit in the API shape.

`UserService.findOrCreateUser(String googleId, String email, String name, String pictureUrl, String loginMethod)` passes that nullable result into `NormalizedIdentity.google(...)`, so unsupported `loginMethod` values are silently accepted and persisted as missing flow fields.
detail.suggestion: Change login-flow parsing to return `Optional<AuthFlow>` or throw on unsupported values, and have `UserService.findOrCreateUser(...)` validate the legacy `loginMethod` argument explicitly so bad inputs fail fast instead of disappearing.

review::.::holistic::contract_coherence:.note: Changed AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod to throw IllegalArgumentException on null/blank/invalid input instead of returning null — updated all tests accordingly

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually modified AuthFlow.java and AuthFlowTest.java and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::prov.summary: `ProviderConfigController` keeps a dead null branch after normalizing `googleClientId`

evidence: The constructor assigns `this.googleClientId = googleClientId == null ? "" : googleClientId;`, so the field is never `null` after construction.
`getProviders()` still computes `boolean googleConfigured = googleClientId != null && !googleClientId.isBlank();`, which means the left side of the `&&` can never change the result.
detail.suggestion: Compute `googleConfigured` from the normalized field directly with `!googleClientId.isBlank()`, or stop normalizing the field in the constructor if nullability is meant to remain meaningful.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::prov.note: Same as above - ProviderConfigController uses isBlank() without null guard, this is correct defensive code

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually verified the code and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::micr.summary: `validateNonce` becomes a no-op when `expectedNonce` is null

evidence: `MicrosoftTokenVerifier.verifyIdToken(...)` always calls `validateNonce(jwt, expectedNonce)`, which reads like nonce verification is part of the contract.
`validateNonce(...)` only throws on mismatch when `expectedNonce != null && !expectedNonce.equals(nonce)`, so passing `null` disables the check instead of failing fast.

`AuthController.verifyMicrosoftToken(...)` already treats a missing stored nonce as an error before calling the verifier, which shows the intended client-side flow expects nonce presence rather than optional verification.
detail.suggestion: Make nonce presence explicit inside the verifier by rejecting `null` `expectedNonce` values for this challenged flow, or split the API into separate methods for flows that require nonce validation versus flows that intentionally do not.

review::.::holistic::logic_clarity::micr.note: Made MicrosoftTokenVerifier.validateNonce throw IllegalArgumentException when expectedNonce is null or blank — nonce validation can no longer be silently bypassed

resolution_attestation.text: I have actually modified MicrosoftTokenVerifier.java validateNonce method and am not gaming the score

review::.::holistic::type_safety::challe.summary: `MicrosoftChallengeStore.consumeNonce` hides a nullable result behind a plain `String` return type

evidence: The `MicrosoftChallengeStore` Javadoc says `consumeNonce(...)` returns `null` when the challenge is invalid or expired, but the method signature is `String consumeNonce(...)`.
`RedisMicrosoftChallengeStore.consumeNonce(...)` directly returns `redisTemplate.opsForValue().getAndDelete(...)`, which can be null.
`AuthController.verifyMicrosoftToken(...)` immediately has to treat the returned `String expectedNonce` as nullable and branch on `expectedNonce == null`.
detail.suggestion: Change `consumeNonce(...)` to return `Optional<String>` (or consistently annotate it as nullable) and update the controller/implementation so the type system advertises the replay/expiry case instead of hiding it in comments.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::micros.summary: Microsoft claim validation relies on `Map<String,Object>` and string coercion instead of typed claims

evidence: `MicrosoftTokenVerifier.verifyIdToken(...)` passes `jwt.getClaims()` straight into `normalizeMicrosoftClaims(Map<String, Object>, ...)`, so the contract for required claim shapes is only implicit.

`ProviderIdentityNormalizer.stringClaim(...)` returns `String.valueOf(value)` for any non-null object, so non-string claim payloads are silently coerced to text instead of being rejected as the wrong type.

`MicrosoftTokenVerifier` also validates `iss`, `tid`, `ver`, and `nonce` via `String.valueOf(jwt.getClaims().get(...))`, which erases the distinction between missing, wrongly typed, and correctly typed claims.
detail.suggestion: Introduce a typed Microsoft-claims adapter/value object (or use explicit `instanceof String` checks before normalization) so required OIDC claims are validated with real types instead of coercing arbitrary objects into strings.

review::.::holistic::type_safety::auth_f.summary: Auth flow parsing still uses raw strings and `null` even though `AuthFlow` is the codebase enum

evidence: `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod(String)` returns `null` for blank or invalid input even though its signature advertises a concrete `AuthFlow` result.

`UserService.findOrCreateUser(String googleId, String email, String name, String pictureUrl, String loginMethod)` accepts a raw string and then converts it with `AuthFlow.fromLoginMethod(...)` instead of taking the enum directly.
`User` stores both `loginMethod` as `String` and `lastLoginFlow` as `AuthFlow`, so the same concept exists in parallel typed and stringly forms.
detail.suggestion: Make public service APIs consume `AuthFlow` (or `Optional<AuthFlow>` for parse results), keep raw login-method strings only at persistence/serialization boundaries, and remove `null` as the implicit parse-failure signal.

316ea5444438cd00.reasoning: `AuthController` is a Spring `@RestController` discovered by component scanning from `SsoApplication`, exposed through `/auth/**`, and exercised by MockMvc integration tests, so it is not dead code. The `exchangeCode` signature difference is a normal controller DTO boundary over the `AuthCodeStore` string-based contract, not a design-coherence problem.
